lessons.title: The Routing Table, Connected & Static Routes
lessons.content:
A router's only job at the network layer is, per packet, to answer one question: *given this destination IP, which next hop and out-interface do I use?* The answer lives in the **routing table** (the RIB, Routing Information Base, which is distilled into the FIB the data plane actually consults). Each entry carries five things worth memorizing: the **destination prefix** (e.g. `10.1.2.0/24`), the **next-hop** address (the neighbor to hand the packet to), the **outgoing interface**, a **metric** (cost within a given routing source), and the **administrative distance** (AD, how much the router trusts the *source* of the route).

Routes arrive from three origins. **Connected** routes appear automatically when you assign an IP to an interface — the router knows that subnet is directly attached (AD 0). **Static** routes are entered by hand (`ip route add 10.2.0.0/16 via 192.0.2.1`, AD 1); they never change unless you change them. **Dynamic** routes are learned from a protocol (OSPF, BGP, ...) that adapts to topology automatically.

The **default route**, `0.0.0.0/0` (`::/0` in IPv6), matches *everything* — it is the "if nothing more specific matched, send it here" catch-all, typically pointing at your upstream gateway.

The selection rule is **longest-prefix match**: among all entries that match the destination, the one with the most specific (longest) mask wins — *independent of metric or AD*. A `/32` host route beats a `/24`, which beats the `/0` default. AD and metric only break ties between routes of equal prefix length, choosing first the most-trusted source, then the lowest metric within it.

items.front: Name the five fields of a routing-table entry.
items.back: Destination prefix, next-hop address, outgoing interface, metric (cost within a routing source), and administrative distance (trust in the route's source).
items.hint: Where to, via whom, out what, how far, how trusted.

items.front: When multiple routes match a destination, which one is used?
items.back: The one with the longest (most specific) prefix mask — longest-prefix match. This is decided before metric or administrative distance ever enter the picture.

items.front: What is administrative distance (AD), and when does it matter?
items.back: AD ranks how much a router trusts the SOURCE of a route (connected 0 < static 1 < eBGP 20 < OSPF 110 < RIP 120). It only decides between routes to the same prefix learned from different sources — it never overrides longest-prefix match.
items.hint: Trust between protocols, not a path cost.

items.front: What does the default route 0.0.0.0/0 do?
items.back: It matches every destination with prefix length 0, so it is chosen only when no more-specific route matches. It is the catch-all 'gateway of last resort,' usually pointing at the upstream router.
items.hint: /0 matches all, but is the shortest prefix.

items.front: Distinguish a connected route from a static route.
items.back: A connected route is installed automatically when an interface gets an IP/subnet (the router is directly attached, AD 0). A static route is configured manually by an operator for a remote prefix (AD 1) and persists unchanged until edited.
items.hint: Auto from the interface vs typed by hand.

items.prompt: A router has routes 0.0.0.0/0, 10.0.0.0/8, 10.1.0.0/16, and 10.1.2.0/24. A packet is destined for 10.1.2.130. Which route is used?
choices: 0.0.0.0/0, because the default route is always preferred
10.0.0.0/8, because it has the lowest metric
10.1.2.0/24, because it is the longest matching prefix
10.1.0.0/16, because /16 is the most common LAN size
items.explanation: All four entries match 10.1.2.130, but forwarding uses longest-prefix match, so the most specific /24 wins regardless of metric or default status.

items.prompt: For which network is static routing typically the BEST choice?
choices: A large carrier backbone with thousands of changing prefixes
A small stub network with a single uplink and a stable topology
A multi-homed network that must steer traffic by policy
A datacenter fabric needing sub-second reconvergence
items.explanation: Static routes shine when topology is small, stable, and predictable (a stub with one exit). Large, dynamic, or multi-homed environments need a dynamic protocol to scale and adapt.

items.prompt: Two routes to 10.5.0.0/16 exist: one via OSPF (AD 110, metric 20) and one static (AD 1, metric 0). Which is installed?
choices: The OSPF route, because OSPF computes optimal paths
The static route, because it has the lower administrative distance
Both, load-balanced equally
Neither; the conflict invalidates both routes
items.explanation: Same prefix length, so AD decides: static (1) is more trusted than OSPF (110). Metric only compares routes from the same source, so it is irrelevant across different ADs.

items.prompt: Why does a directly-connected route normally win over any learned route to the same subnet?
choices: It has the highest metric
It has administrative distance 0, the most trusted source
Connected routes use a longer prefix by definition
Dynamic protocols never advertise local subnets
items.explanation: Connected routes have AD 0 — nothing is more trustworthy than 'this subnet is physically on my interface,' so they outrank static and dynamic routes to the same prefix.

lessons.title: Dynamic Routing & Interior Gateway Protocols
lessons.content:
Static routes do not scale or self-heal, so larger networks run **dynamic routing protocols** that discover topology and recompute paths automatically. There are three algorithmic families.

**Distance-vector** protocols advertise only *distance and direction* ("I can reach X at cost N, via me") to direct neighbors — routing by rumor. **RIP** is the classic example: metric is raw **hop count**, max 15 (16 = unreachable). Its great weakness is **count-to-infinity**: when a network goes down, routers can bounce inflated metrics back and forth, converging only by slowly counting up to 16. Split horizon, route poisoning, and hold-down timers patch this, but RIP is essentially **historical** today.

**Link-state** protocols are smarter. Each router floods a description of its own links — **LSAs** (Link-State Advertisements) — so every router builds an identical map of the whole area, then runs **Dijkstra's SPF** algorithm locally to compute shortest paths. **OSPF** is the dominant **IGP** (interior gateway protocol): it uses **areas** (a backbone area 0 plus others) to bound LSA flooding and scale, with cost based on link bandwidth. **IS-IS** is the other major link-state IGP — algorithmically very similar to OSPF but riding directly on L2 rather than IP, favored by **large carriers** for its scalability and protocol-agnostic design.

**EIGRP** is Cisco's advanced distance-vector ("hybrid") protocol: it keeps DV's neighbor-based advertisement but adds the **DUAL** algorithm with precomputed feasible successors for near-instant, loop-free failover, plus a composite bandwidth/delay metric.

Key distinction: an **IGP** (OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP, RIP) optimizes paths *inside one administrative domain*, where the goal is genuinely shortest/lowest-cost path. That goal changes completely at the edge between organizations — which is BGP's world (next lesson).

items.front: Name the three dynamic-routing algorithm families and one protocol of each.
items.back: Distance-vector (RIP, EIGRP), link-state (OSPF, IS-IS), and path-vector (BGP). DV advertises distance+direction to neighbors; link-state floods a full topology map; path-vector advertises the full AS path.
items.hint: Rumor, map, and full route history.

items.front: What is the count-to-infinity problem, and where does it occur?
items.back: In distance-vector protocols like RIP, a lost route's metric can be re-learned and incremented in a loop between neighbors, climbing slowly toward 'infinity' (16 hops in RIP) before converging. Split horizon, poison reverse, and hold-down timers mitigate it.

Each router floods Link-State Advertisements (LSAs) describing its links; every router assembles an identical link-state database (a topology map) for its area, then runs Dijkstra's SPF algorithm locally to find shortest paths by cumulative link cost.
items.hint: Flood links, build map, run Dijkstra.

items.back: Areas bound the scope of LSA flooding and SPF computation so the topology database stays small as the network grows. All areas attach to the backbone area 0; inter-area routes pass through it, improving scalability and stability.
items.hint: Divide the domain; backbone is area 0.

items.front: What distinguishes IS-IS and EIGRP from OSPF?
items.back: IS-IS is a link-state IGP like OSPF but runs directly over L2 (not IP) and scales well, so large carriers favor it. EIGRP is Cisco's advanced distance-vector protocol using the DUAL algorithm and feasible successors for fast, loop-free convergence.
items.hint: Carrier link-state vs Cisco hybrid DV.

items.prompt: What is the primary design goal of an interior gateway protocol (IGP) such as OSPF or IS-IS?
choices: Enforce business policy between competing companies
Find the lowest-cost (shortest) path within a single administrative domain
Translate private addresses to public ones
Assign IP addresses to hosts automatically
items.explanation: An IGP runs inside one organization where everyone shares the goal of optimal forwarding, so it legitimately optimizes for shortest/lowest-cost path. Inter-organization policy is BGP's job.

items.prompt: Which statement best contrasts link-state with distance-vector protocols?
choices: Distance-vector routers each build a full topology map; link-state routers do not
Link-state routers flood link info and each runs SPF on a full map; distance-vector routers learn only neighbors' distances
Link-state protocols cannot be used as IGPs
Distance-vector protocols converge faster because they hold the whole topology
items.explanation: Link-state floods LSAs so every router has the same complete map and runs Dijkstra locally; distance-vector only hears neighbors' advertised distances ('routing by rumor'), which is what makes it prone to count-to-infinity.

items.prompt: RIP is considered largely historical primarily because:
choices: Its 15-hop diameter limit and slow count-to-infinity convergence don't suit modern networks
It cannot route IPv4 traffic at all
It requires a centralized controller
It uses Dijkstra's algorithm, which is too slow
items.explanation: RIP caps usable paths at 15 hops and converges slowly via count-to-infinity. Modern networks use link-state (OSPF/IS-IS) or EIGRP for scale and fast convergence. RIP does not use Dijkstra; that's link-state.

items.prompt: EIGRP achieves fast, loop-free failover mainly through:
choices: Flooding LSAs to every router and rerunning Dijkstra
The DUAL algorithm with precomputed feasible successors (backup next hops)
Counting hop count up to infinity
Centralizing the control plane in a controller
items.explanation: EIGRP's DUAL maintains feasible successors — already-validated loop-free backup routes — so when the primary fails it can switch instantly without recomputation or risk of loops.

lessons.title: BGP, Policy, and the Control/Data Plane
lessons.content:
The Internet is not one network but ~tens of thousands of **autonomous systems (AS)** — independently operated networks (ISPs, clouds, enterprises), each with an **AS number**. **BGP** (Border Gateway Protocol) is the single **path-vector** protocol that stitches them together; it is, functionally, the routing protocol that *is* the Internet.

A path-vector advertisement carries the full **AS-PATH** — the ordered list of ASes a prefix traversed. Receivers reject any path that already contains their own AS, which is how BGP prevents loops without a global map. **eBGP** runs *between* different ASes (the actual inter-domain glue); **iBGP** carries those externally-learned routes *across* the interior of a single AS so all border routers agree.

The crucial idea: **BGP is policy routing, not shortest-path routing.** It would be wrong for BGP to simply pick the fewest-AS-hop path, because routing between organizations is about *money and contracts*, not distance. An ISP prefers to send traffic over a customer link (it gets paid) before a peer (free) before a transit provider (it pays), regardless of AS-PATH length. BGP path attributes — LOCAL_PREF, AS_PATH length, MED, communities — encode exactly this business policy. That is the deep answer to *why the Internet needs policy, not just shortest path*: the metric an operator cares about is economic and contractual, which no IGP cost can express.

Because BGP is built on trust and accepts what neighbors announce, a single fat-fingered config can be catastrophic. A **route leak** or **hijack** — announcing prefixes you don't own, or a more-specific prefix that wins longest-prefix match globally — can blackhole or reroute large swaths of the Internet, the cause of several famous **global outages**.

Underpinning all of this is the **control plane vs data plane** split: the control plane (BGP, OSPF, etc.) *builds* the table; the data plane *forwards* packets per-table at line rate. **Convergence** is how long after a change all routers agree; **route flapping** (a link bouncing up/down) churns the control plane, so **dampening** suppresses unstable routes. Finally, **ECMP** (equal-cost multipath) lets the data plane hash flows across several equal-cost next hops for load sharing.

items.front: What kind of protocol is BGP, and what does its key attribute carry?
items.back: BGP is a path-vector protocol. Each advertisement carries the AS-PATH — the full ordered list of autonomous systems the prefix passed through. A router rejects any path containing its own AS number, which prevents routing loops.
items.hint: It advertises the whole route's history, by AS.

items.back: eBGP runs between routers in DIFFERENT autonomous systems — the actual inter-domain glue of the Internet. iBGP runs between routers WITHIN the same AS to distribute externally-learned routes so all the AS's border routers share a consistent view.
items.hint: Between ASes vs inside one AS.

items.front: Why does the Internet need policy routing instead of just shortest-path routing?
items.back: Routing between organizations is governed by money and contracts, not distance. An ISP prefers customer links (paid) over peers (free) over transit (costs money) even if the AS-PATH is longer. Economic/contractual preference can't be expressed as an IGP cost, so BGP uses policy attributes (LOCAL_PREF, MED, communities).

items.front: Differentiate the control plane from the data plane.
items.back: The control plane runs routing protocols (BGP, OSPF) to BUILD the forwarding table. The data plane uses that table to FORWARD each packet at line rate. Routing = building the map; forwarding = following it per-packet.
items.hint: Build the table vs follow the table.

items.front: Define convergence, route flapping/dampening, and ECMP.
items.back: Convergence is the time for all routers to agree after a topology change. Flapping is a route repeatedly going up/down; dampening suppresses such unstable routes to protect the control plane. ECMP (equal-cost multipath) load-shares traffic across multiple equal-cost next hops.
items.hint: Settle, suppress instability, and split load.

items.prompt: How does BGP prevent routing loops between autonomous systems?
choices: It uses a TTL that decrements at each AS
It runs Dijkstra's SPF on a global topology map
A router rejects any advertised path whose AS-PATH already contains its own AS number
It limits the metric to a maximum of 15 hops
items.explanation: As a path-vector protocol, BGP attaches the full AS-PATH. If a router sees its own AS already in the path, the route would loop, so it discards it. No global map (link-state) or hop cap (RIP) is involved.

items.prompt: Why can a single BGP misconfiguration cause a global Internet outage?
choices: BGP encrypts all traffic, so one bad key breaks everything
BGP largely trusts what neighbors announce, so a bad/more-specific or leaked prefix can be accepted and propagated worldwide, blackholing or rerouting traffic
BGP recomputes every router's table from scratch each second
BGP has no way to aggregate routes, so tables overflow instantly
items.explanation: BGP accepts announcements on trust. A hijack or leak — especially a more-specific prefix that wins longest-prefix match — can be propagated globally, sending huge volumes of traffic to the wrong AS or a black hole. This has caused several real worldwide incidents.

items.prompt: What is the fundamental difference between an IGP and BGP?
choices: An IGP works only on IPv6; BGP works only on IPv4
An IGP optimizes shortest path within one administrative domain; BGP enforces policy between independently-operated autonomous systems
BGP is a link-state protocol; an IGP is always path-vector
An IGP needs a central controller; BGP is fully distributed
items.explanation: IGPs (OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP, RIP) run inside one org and genuinely seek the lowest-cost path. BGP runs between autonomous systems where decisions are driven by contracts and policy, not raw distance — the core IGP-vs-BGP distinction.

items.prompt: What does ECMP (equal-cost multipath) accomplish in the data plane?
choices: It dampens flapping routes to protect the control plane
It distributes flows across multiple next hops that have equal cost, sharing load while keeping each flow's packets in order
It guarantees a single deterministic path for every packet
It converts iBGP routes into eBGP routes
items.explanation: When several next hops tie on cost, ECMP hashes traffic across them (typically per-flow, to preserve packet ordering) for load sharing and resilience — a data-plane behavior, distinct from control-plane dampening.
